section.title: Eliphaz's First Speech
section.sectionRationale: Eliphaz breaks the seven days of silence and opens the first of three cycles of dialogue. He is the gentlest of the friends, appealing to Job's own past wisdom, a mystical night vision, and genuinely true statements about human sinfulness and divine discipline. But his underlying principle, that suffering proves sin and prosperity proves righteousness, will be applied to Job with increasing cruelty by the friends as the book goes on, and it is this very principle that God will rebuke at the book's end.
section.thesis: Eliphaz's first speech mixes real wisdom, that no mortal is pure before God and that God's discipline of His people is good, with a rigid principle applied wrongly to Job: that his suffering must be traced to some fault, when the reader already knows it was not.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Eliphaz's gentle opening appeal to Job's own past counsel; his stated principle that the innocent do not perish; a night vision revealing that no mortal is pure before God; his observation that man is born to trouble; his counsel to appeal to God, who does great and unsearchable things; and his closing promise that the man God corrects will be restored.

units.label: A gentle beginning
units.note: your words have steadied those who stumbled

verses.text: Then Eliphaz the Temanite replied:
"If one ventures a word with you, will you be wearied? Yet who can keep from speaking?
Surely you have instructed many, and have strengthened their feeble hands.
Your words have steadied those who stumbled; you have braced the knees that were buckling.
But now trouble has come upon you, and you are weary. It strikes you, and you are dismayed.
Is your reverence not your confidence, and the {{yosher|uprightness}} of your ways your hope?

units.label: Who, being innocent, has perished?
units.note: those who plow iniquity reap the same

verses.text:
Consider now, I plead: 
Who, being innocent, has ever perished? Or where have the upright been destroyed?
As I have observed, those who plow iniquity and those who sow trouble reap the same.
By the breath of {{eloah|God}} they perish, and by the blast of His anger they are consumed.
The lion may roar, and the fierce lion may growl, yet the teeth of the young lions are broken.
The old lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness are scattered.

units.label: A vision in the night
units.note: can a mortal be more righteous than God

verses.text: Now a word came to me secretly; my ears caught a whisper of it.
In disquieting visions in the night, when deep sleep falls on men,
fear and trembling came over me and made all my bones shudder.
Then a spirit glided past my face, and the hair on my body bristled.
It stood still, but I could not discern its appearance; a form loomed before my eyes, and I heard a whispering voice:
'Can a mortal be more righteous than God, or a man more pure than his Maker?
If God puts no trust in His servants, and He charges His angels with error,

how much more those who dwell in houses of clay, whose foundations are in the dust, 
who can be crushed like a moth!
They are smashed to pieces from dawn to dusk; unnoticed, they perish forever.
Are not their tent cords pulled up, so that they die without wisdom?'

units.label: Man is born to trouble
units.note: as surely as sparks fly upward

verses.text: "Call out if you please, but who will answer? To which of the holy ones will you turn?
For resentment kills a fool, and envy slays the simple.
I have seen a fool taking root, but suddenly his house was cursed.
His sons are far from safety, crushed in court without a defender.

The hungry consume his harvest, taking it even from the thorns, 
and the thirsty pant after his wealth.
For distress does not spring from the dust, and trouble does not sprout from the ground.
Yet man is born to trouble as surely as sparks fly upward.

units.label: Appeal to God, who does great things

verses.text: However, if I were you, I would appeal to God and lay my cause before Him,
the One who does great and unsearchable things, wonders without number.
He gives rain to the earth and sends water upon the fields.
He sets the lowly on high, so that mourners are lifted to safety.
He thwarts the schemes of the crafty, so that their hands find no success.
He catches the wise in their craftiness, and sweeps away the plans of the cunning.
They encounter darkness by day and grope at noon as in the night.
He saves the needy from the sword in their mouth and from the clutches of the powerful.
So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth.

units.label: Blessed is the man whom God corrects
units.note: He wounds, but He also binds

verses.text: Blessed indeed is the man whom God corrects; so do not despise the {{musar|discipline}} of the {{shadday|Almighty}}.
For He wounds, but He also binds; He strikes, but His hands also heal.
He will rescue you from six calamities; no harm will touch you in seven.
In famine He will redeem you from death, and in battle from the stroke of the sword.
You will be hidden from the scourge of the tongue, and will not fear havoc when it comes.
You will laugh at destruction and famine, and need not fear the beasts of the earth.
For you will have a covenant with the stones of the field, and the wild animals will be at peace with you.
You will know that your tent is secure, and find nothing amiss when inspecting your home.
You will know that your offspring will be many, your descendants like the grass of the earth.
You will come to the grave in full vigor, like a sheaf of grain gathered in season.
Indeed, we have investigated, and it is true! So hear it and know for yourself."

yosher.gloss: Uprightness, straightness of conduct. Eliphaz appeals to it as the ground of Job's hope, assuming that Job's own uprightness should now be enough to secure his deliverance, an assumption the rest of the book will complicate rather than confirm.

eloah.gloss: God, a poetic singular form used far more often in Job than anywhere else in the Old Testament, favored in this book's elevated speech instead of the covenant name YHWH. Its frequent use across all the speeches, friends and Job alike, marks Job as addressing the God of all humanity, not only the God of Israel's covenant history.

musar.gloss: Discipline, correction, moral instruction, the same word that anchors much of the book of Proverbs. Eliphaz's claim that Job's suffering is God's musar assumes it functions as fatherly correction for sin; whether that assumption fits Job's actual case is exactly what the book contests.

shadday.gloss: The Almighty, a name for God used with striking frequency throughout Job, emphasizing overwhelming power. Both Job and his friends use this name freely; the dispute in the book is never over whether God is almighty, but over how that almighty power relates to human suffering.

basic.context: Eliphaz speaks first among the friends, and gently. He reminds Job that Job himself has comforted others in trouble before, then states his core conviction: the innocent do not perish, and those who sow trouble reap it. He backs this up with a mysterious night vision in which a spirit tells him no mortal can be pure before God. He observes that man is born to trouble as sparks fly upward, but counsels Job to appeal to God, who does wondrous things and lifts up the lowly. He closes with a genuinely beautiful promise: that the one God disciplines will be restored, protected, and blessed with long life.
basic.hermeneutics: Much of what Eliphaz says here is true in general and even quotable, no mortal is pure before God, God's discipline of His own is good, God does wondrous things for the humble. The error is not in these general truths but in the specific application: Eliphaz assumes Job's catastrophic suffering must be correction for some fault, when the reader already knows from chapters 1 and 2 that it is not. This is the book's first demonstration that true words, wrongly applied to a particular sufferer, can wound rather than heal.
basic.originalLanguages: Eliphaz uses eloah and shadday, poetic and powerful names for God favored throughout this book, and appeals to Job's yosher, uprightness, as though it alone should secure deliverance. His counsel that Job's suffering is God's musar, discipline, assumes a direct link between suffering and correction for fault, the very link the whole book will complicate.
basic.typology: Eliphaz's true but misapplied words anticipate the danger of proof-texting suffering, applying real biblical truths carelessly to specific people in pain, a temptation the church has faced in every generation since. His promise of restoration after discipline, though wrongly timed for Job, does anticipate the true pattern of discipline and restoration God brings to His children in Christ.
basic.christ: Eliphaz's vision declares that no mortal is pure before God, not even His angels; the whole book will eventually show that no human wisdom, however sincere, is sufficient to explain suffering rightly. Only Christ, truly righteous and yet willingly afflicted though guiltless, breaks the assumed link between suffering and personal fault that Eliphaz cannot see past. Where Eliphaz promises the disciplined will be restored to prosperity, Christ promises something greater: resurrection life for all who are united to Him, whether or not restoration comes in this life.
basic.god: Eliphaz's God is real and rightly described in many particulars: powerful, just, the one who lifts the lowly and disciplines those He loves. But Eliphaz's God is also too small in this speech, boxed into a formula that cannot account for Job's actual situation, a warning about how easily true doctrine can be twisted into a rigid system.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 12:5-11 affirms that God does discipline those He loves, while also insisting no discipline seems pleasant at the time; Romans 3:10 echoes the vision's claim that none is righteous, no not one; John 9:2-3 shows Jesus explicitly rejecting the assumption that suffering always traces to sin.
basic.oneStory: Eliphaz opens the dialogue with words that sound wise and often are, yet applied wrongly to an innocent sufferer they become part of the very failure God will rebuke at the book's end. His speech teaches the church to handle true doctrine carefully, since even sound words can wound when spoken without knowledge of the actual case before us.

context.literary: This is the first of three speeches Eliphaz gives across the book's three cycles, each one shorter and harsher than the last; here, at its gentlest, it still lays down the retribution principle that will govern and eventually break down across the whole dialogue.
context.historical: Eliphaz is a Temanite, from Edomite territory associated in the Old Testament with proverbial wisdom, Jeremiah 49:7, fitting his role here as the wisdom-tradition voice among the three friends, appealing to observation, tradition, and even mystical experience as sources of authority.
context.canonical: The book of Proverbs' confident link between wisdom, righteousness, and prosperity provides the theological background Eliphaz draws on; Job as a whole, alongside Ecclesiastes, supplies Scripture's necessary complication of that same wisdom tradition, showing it does not hold without exception.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the first, gentlest form of the friends' central error, that suffering proves fault, so that the reader can see both its surface plausibility and its ultimate wrongness when applied to an innocent man.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Eliphaz's speech accurately and inspired Scripture records it faithfully, but it does not thereby endorse his application to Job as prescriptive truth; God's later verdict in 42:7 that the friends did not speak rightly about Him retroactively marks this speech's central application as an error, even where many of its individual statements remain true.

debates.question: How can Eliphaz's speech contain real truths yet still be wrong?

views.label: The truths are wrongly applied to Job's specific case
views.case: Statements like 'no mortal is pure before God' and 'God disciplines those He loves' are sound doctrine in general; Eliphaz's error is applying them to conclude that Job's suffering specifically proves some fault, which the reader knows is false.

views.label: The truths themselves are incomplete, needing the fuller picture the book will supply
views.case: Even taken generally, Eliphaz's principle that the innocent never perish, 4:7, is too absolute; the rest of Scripture, including Job's own life, shows innocent suffering is real, so his framework needs correcting, not merely his application of it.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter warns readers against treating any general spiritual principle, however true it sounds, as an infallible explanation for a specific person's suffering.

study.originalLanguages: Eliphaz favors eloah and shadday, the elevated divine names common throughout Job's poetry, and grounds his counsel in Job's yosher, uprightness, and in musar, discipline, assuming suffering functions as corrective discipline for fault. These are genuine biblical categories; the book's tension lies in whether they explain Job's particular case.

typology.type: The claim that no mortal is pure before God, not even His angels
typology.fulfillment: Romans 3:10, none is righteous, answered fully only in the righteousness of Christ credited to sinners.

typology.type: The promise that the one God disciplines will finally be restored
typology.fulfillment: Hebrews 12:11, the discipline of the Lord yielding the peaceful fruit of righteousness, fulfilled in the resurrection life secured in Christ.

study.christ: Eliphaz's vision rightly declares no mortal pure before God, but wrongly assumes this means Job's suffering must trace to specific fault. Christ alone answers the deeper problem the vision raises: since no one, not even angels, stands pure before God on their own merit, only a righteousness given from outside ourselves can suffice, and that righteousness is found in Christ, who was Himself afflicted though entirely without the fault Eliphaz keeps searching for in Job.
study.god: God is rightly praised here as the doer of great and unsearchable things, who lifts the lowly and disciplines those He loves; nothing Eliphaz says of God's character in general is false. What is false is the confidence that this God's ways can be reduced to a predictable formula that always explains present suffering by past sin.

crossReferences.note: God disciplines those He loves, though no discipline seems pleasant at the time; affirms Eliphaz's principle while insisting it does not remove all pain from the process.
None is righteous, no not one, echoing the vision's claim that no mortal is pure before God.
Jesus explicitly rejects the assumption, embedded in Eliphaz's whole argument, that suffering always indicates personal or ancestral sin.

study.oneStory: Eliphaz's gentle first speech shows how true words about God's holiness, discipline, and care can still wound when wrongly applied to an innocent sufferer. The book uses his very real wisdom to expose a deeper need: a righteousness not built on the absence of suffering but on Christ, who bore suffering though truly without the fault Eliphaz cannot stop assuming.

quiz.q: What is the central principle Eliphaz states in 4:7-8?

options.text: That the innocent do not perish, and those who sow trouble reap the same, implying suffering proves guilt.
options.note: This retribution principle drives Eliphaz's whole argument and will recur, hardening, throughout the friends' later speeches.

options.text: That those who plow iniquity and sow trouble will eventually reap what they have sown.
options.note: A true restatement of half the principle, but less complete than the full claim that innocence itself guarantees safety from perishing.

options.text: That only the wicked ever experience prosperity.
options.note: Eliphaz's principle runs the other direction: prosperity for the righteous and calamity for the wicked, not exclusive prosperity for the wicked.

options.text: That suffering is entirely random and unrelated to anyone's conduct.
options.note: This is the opposite of Eliphaz's actual claim, which strongly links conduct and consequence.

quiz.q: What does the mysterious night vision in 4:12-21 declare?

options.text: That no mortal can be more righteous than God, and even His angels are charged with error, so how much less those who dwell in houses of clay.
options.note: The vision's message in verses 17 to 19 makes this universal claim about human and even angelic frailty before God.

options.text: That every human being, being made of dust, falls short of God's purity.
options.note: Captures the vision's basic thrust about human frailty, though less complete than its fuller argument from angels down to mortals.

options.text: That angels are entirely free from any possibility of error.
options.note: The vision says the opposite, that God charges even His angels with error, as part of its argument from greater to lesser.

options.text: That Job specifically has committed a great sin.
options.note: The vision speaks in general terms about mortals and angels; it does not name any specific sin of Job's.

quiz.q: What promise does Eliphaz make in 5:17-27 to the one God disciplines?

options.text: That such a person will be rescued from calamity, secure in their home, blessed with many descendants, and will come to the grave in full vigor.
options.note: This is the sweeping restoration Eliphaz promises, assuming Job's submission to correction will reverse his losses.

options.text: That God wounds but also binds up, so that real healing and blessing eventually follow real discipline.
options.note: A true summary of Eliphaz's wounds-and-binds pattern, though less complete than his full, sweeping list of promised blessings.

options.text: That God's discipline never actually wounds, only heals.
options.note: Eliphaz explicitly says God wounds and also binds, strikes and also heals; the wounding is real, not denied.

options.text: That they will suffer even more severely as further correction.
options.note: Eliphaz promises the opposite outcome, restoration and blessing, not escalating suffering.

quiz.q: Why does the reader have reason to be cautious about Eliphaz's speech, even though much of it sounds true?

options.text: Because the reader already knows from chapters 1 and 2 that Job's suffering was not caused by any sin, so Eliphaz's application of his true general principles to Job's specific case is mistaken.
options.note: This is the central irony the narrator has set up: the reader has information the characters in the dialogue do not.

options.text: Because Eliphaz assumes his general theological principle must explain Job's specific, unique situation.
options.note: Names the real error, though less precise than pointing to the exact information, chapters 1 and 2, that exposes it.

options.text: Because everything Eliphaz says is entirely false.
options.note: Many of his individual statements, such as God's discipline of those He loves, are true in general; the error lies in the application to Job.

options.text: Because Eliphaz is portrayed as speaking in bad faith.
options.note: Nothing in the text suggests Eliphaz is insincere; he appears to genuinely believe his counsel will help Job.

quiz.q: What later verse in the book directly evaluates speeches like Eliphaz's first one?

options.text: Job 42:7, where God tells Eliphaz that he and his two friends have not spoken of God what is right, as His servant Job has.
options.note: This final verdict retroactively marks the friends' application of their theology, including this speech, as mistaken.

options.text: The book's closing chapters, where God addresses Eliphaz and his companions directly about their speeches.
options.note: Points to the right general location, but less precise than citing 42:7 exactly and its specific wording about not speaking rightly.

options.text: Job 5:27, Eliphaz's own closing claim that his words are true.
options.note: This is Eliphaz's own self-assessment within the speech, not an outside verdict on it; the book's actual judgment comes later, from God.

options.text: Job 1:22, describing Job's response to his first losses.
options.note: That verse evaluates Job's own response to suffering, not Eliphaz's later theological speeches.

questions: Eliphaz begins gently, reminding Job of his own past wisdom, before his counsel eventually turns harder to bear. How do you notice the moment when well-meant counsel starts to wound rather than help, in yourself or others?

Much of what Eliphaz says about God, His discipline, His care for the humble, His unsearchable greatness, is true. What true things about God have you heard misapplied to your own suffering or someone else's in a way that hurt rather than healed?

Eliphaz assumes Job's suffering must trace to some fault. Where are you tempted to look for a tidy explanation for pain, yours or another's, rather than sit with the honest possibility that some suffering has no such explanation?

Eliphaz's promise of restoration in 5:17-27 is beautiful and largely true of God's ways in general. How do you hold real hope for God's future goodness without turning it into a demand that He restore things on your own timeline?

God's later rebuke of the friends reminds us that even sincere, doctrinally careful counsel can still be wrong. When you comfort someone who is suffering, what would it look like to hold your own explanations more loosely?

translationNotes.note: The uprightness of your ways your hope reflects Eliphaz's underlying assumption that righteous conduct should function as a guarantee against calamity, an assumption the book as a whole contests.

By the breath of God renders a phrase using eloah, the poetic singular divine name that dominates Job's speeches far more than the covenant name YHWH, common in narrative sections of the Old Testament.
Discipline renders musar, the same key term used throughout Proverbs for corrective, formative instruction; Eliphaz assumes it explains Job's suffering, an assumption God will later correct.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 4:1-5:27. The em dash at the end of 5:8 in the source text was replaced with a comma per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
